en.extract:
The Indochinese green magpie (Cissa hypoleuca), also known as the yellow-breasted magpie, is a small colourful bird native to the forests of China and Vietnam.


Taxonomy
The Indochinese green magpie was formally described in 1885 by the Italian zoologists Tommaso Salvadori and Enrico Hillyer Giglioli under the current binomial name Cissa hypoleuca based on a specimen collected in Thủ Dầu Một near Ho Chi Minh City in southern Vietnam. The specific epithet hypoleuca combines the Ancient Greek ὑπο/hupo meaning "beneath" with λευκος/leukos meaning "white".
The genus Cissa includes three other short-tailed magpies species: the common green magpie (Cissa chinensis), the Javan green magpie (Cissa thalassina), and the Bornean green magpie (Cissa jefferyi). The name Cissa is derived from the Ancient Greek kissa which means a "jay" or "magpie" and the genus was discovered by Friedrich Boie in 1826. They all look very similar with their green plumage, iridescent red bill and black mask. They are part of the Corvidae family with crows, ravens, jays, amongst many others. As a perching bird, it is in the order Passeriformes, which includes more than half of all bird species.


Subspecies
Five subspecies are recognised:

C. h. jini Delacour, 1930 – central south China
C. h. concolor Delacour & Jabouille, 1928 – north Vietnam
C. h. chauleti Delacour, 1926 – central Vietnam
C. h. hypoleuca Salvadori & Giglioli, 1885 – southeast Thailand and south Indochina
C. h. katsumatae Rothschild, 1903 – Hainan Island (off southeast China)


Description

This small bird is approximately 35 cm (14 in) in length and has a unique fluorescent plumage just like the other members of the Cissa genus. However, its body is mostly green and those with an underbelly of yellow color are unique to the southern populations in Southeast Asia with the exception of the Chinese population that still have green underbellies, just like the other Cissa birds. They have a black band that goes from their bill, over their crimson eyes, and to the back of their head, that looks similar to the mask of the popular movie character Zorro. They have long reddish-brown flight feathers, and a bill and legs that are brightly red colored. Both sexes look mostly similar, but the juveniles have duller colors than the adults. Duller colors can also be seen when the birds are exposed to too much sunlight.


Distribution and habitat
The yellow-breasted magpie is native to Asia, and can be found in China, Laos, Thailand and Vietnam. It can tolerate altitudes of up to 1,500 meters (4,900 ft) and they span over a region of about 1,920,000 km2 (740,000 sq mi). They stay in these areas all year long and do not migrate.
It can be found in moist forests within the tropical and subtropical regions of their distribution. Due to their bright green plumage, they can be seen easily when jumping from branch to branch in foliage. Therefore this is why they mainly occur in the tree canopies of forests, as this camouflages their green plumage better, protecting them from predation.


Behavior
This passerine bird is very loud and loves to hang out in the foliage of its forest habitat. They have been spotted alone, in pairs, or in small groups. They can be sometimes seen in mixed flocks with laughingthrushes (Garrulax leucolophus) and drongos. The Indochinese green magpie does not migrate and stays in its native range. It has a mainly carnivorous diet and eats lot of different things. An adult Indochinese green magpie once lived for 18 years in captivity. When hunting their prey, they stalk it, corner it and then the male will make the kill.


Vocalizations
Much like other corvids, the Indochinese green magpie is a songbird and has a very good and precise control of its syrinx. It can produce sounds like noisy chattering, rasping notes, screeches, and ringing whistles. They can generate high-pitched series of notes that sounds like "po-puueeee-chuk", followed by lower pitched "eeeoooeeep groak".


Food and feeding
The yellow-breasted magpie can eat a lot of different things, as it is carnivorous. Its diet includes small frogs, insects, eggs of snakes and lizards, and nestlings. The insects from its diet eat a lot of lutein-rich plants, which is a yellow carotenoid pigment. This component is what makes the birds from the Cissa genus go from blue to bright green. Malnourished birds can be found to be more of a blue color due to the lack of the lutein pigment, since its body does not produce it.


Breeding
The month of May appears to be the month when their breeding is recorded. They were found to lay approximately 4 eggs in captivity. They place their nest hidden in a tree at a height of 2–3 meters (6.6–9.8 ft) above the ground. Their nest resembles a bowl made of small branches. Its generation length is of 6.7 years.


Conservation
The Indochinese green magpie has been assessed to be of least concern in 2016 by the IUCN red list. Its current population trend is declining, but the decline is not pronounced enough for them to be considered vulnerable. The decline is caused partially by habitat destruction in Hainan. Another reason would be that the Indochinese green magpie is targeted in the illegal pet trade. Its bright and beautiful colors often makes it an ideal exotic pet, especially as it is very appreciated by birdwatchers. It is difficult to gauge the concurrent status of conservation of the Indochinese green magpie as we have not been able to quantify its global population size.


References


External links

Explore Species: Indochinese Green Magpie at eBird (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)

The '''Indochinese green magpie''' ('''''Cissa hypoleuca'''''), also known as the '''yellow-breasted magpie''', is a small colourful [[bird]] native to the forests of China and Vietnam.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Cissa hypoleuca (Yellow-breasted Magpie) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=63D31D90BF39C75B |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The Indochinese green magpie was [[species description|formally described]] in 1885 by the Italian zoologists [[Tommaso Salvadori]] and [[Enrico Hillyer Giglioli]] under the current [[binomial name]] ''Cissa hypoleuca'' based on a specimen collected in [[Thủ Dầu Một]] near [[Ho Chi Minh City]] in southern Vietnam.<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Salvadori | first1=Tommaso | author1-link=Tommaso Salvadori | last2=Giglioli | first2=Enrico Hillyer | author2-link=Enrico Hillyer Giglioli | date=1885 | title=Due nuovo specie di uccelli della Cocincina raccolta durante il viaggio della R. Pirofregata Magenta | language=Italian | journal=Atti Reale Accademia delle Scienze di Torino | volume=20 | pages=343–345 [343–344] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28773554 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''hypoleuca'' combines the [[Ancient Greek]] {{Lang|grc|ὑπο}}/{{Lang|grc-Latn|hupo}} meaning "beneath" with {{Lang|grc|λευκος}}/{{Lang|grc-Latn|leukos}} meaning "white".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=hypoleuca | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=hypoleuca | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=4 July 2025 }}</ref>

The genus ''Cissa'' includes three other short-tailed magpies species: the [[common green magpie]] (''[[Common green magpie|Cissa chinensis]]'')'','' the [[Javan green magpie]] (''[[Javan green magpie|Cissa thalassina]]'')'','' and the [[Bornean green magpie]] ''([[Bornean green magpie|Cissa jefferyi]]'')''.'' The name ''Cissa'' is derived from the Ancient Greek ''kissa'' which means a "jay" or "magpie" and the genus was discovered by [[Friedrich Boie]] in 1826.<ref>{{Citation |title=Cissa (genus) |date=2023-07-22 |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cissa_(genus)&oldid=1166547562 |work=Wikipedia |access-date=2023-10-19 |language=en}}</ref> They all look very similar with their green plumage, iridescent red bill and black mask. They are part of the ''[[Corvidae]]'' family with crows, ravens, jays, amongst many others. As a [[Passerine|perching bird]], it is in the order [[Passerine|Passeriformes]], which includes more than half of all bird species.<ref>{{Citation |title=Corvidae |date=2023-10-10 |url=https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Corvidae&oldid=1179531989 |work=Wikipedia |access-date=2023-10-19 |language=en}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
Five [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Crows, mudnesters, birds-of-paradise | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/crows/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=4 July 2025}}</ref>

* ''C. h. jini'' [[Jean Théodore Delacour|Delacour]], 1930 – central south China
* ''C. h. concolor'' Delacour & [[Pierre Jabouille|Jabouille]], 1928 – north Vietnam
* ''C. h. chauleti'' Delacour, 1926 – central Vietnam
* ''C. h. hypoleuca'' [[Tommaso Salvadori|Salvadori]] & [[Enrico Hillyer Giglioli|Giglioli]], 1885 – southeast Thailand and south Indochina
* ''C. h. katsumatae'' [[Walter Rothschild, 2nd Baron Rothschild|Rothschild]], 1903 – [[Hainan|Hainan Island]] (off southeast China)

== Description ==
[[File:Indochinese green magpie.jpg|thumb|Normal plumage with yellow underbelly]]
[[File:Cissa hypoleuca.jpg|thumb|Indochinese green magpie]]

This small bird is approximately {{Convert|35|cm|abbr=on}} in length<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oiseaux.net |title=Pirolle à ventre jaune - Cissa hypoleuca - Indochinese Green Magpie |url=https://www.oiseaux.net/birds/indochinese.green.magpie.html |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.oiseaux.net |language=en}}</ref> and has a unique fluorescent plumage just like the other members of the ''[[Cissa (bird)|Cissa]]'' genus. However, its body is mostly green and those with an underbelly of yellow color are unique to the southern populations in Southeast Asia with the exception of the Chinese population that still have green underbellies, just like the other ''Cissa'' birds. They have a black band that goes from their bill, over their crimson eyes, and to the back of their head, that looks similar to the mask of the popular movie character [[Zorro]]. They have long reddish-brown [[flight feather]]s, and a bill and legs that are brightly red colored.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Indochinese Green-Magpie - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/yebmag1 |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> Both sexes look mostly similar, but the juveniles have duller colors than the adults.{{cn|date=February 2025}} Duller colors can also be seen when the birds are exposed to too much sunlight.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Cristo |first1=Roland |last2=Cristo |first2=Ilana |date=1999 |title=Yellow-breasted Hunting Cissa |url=https://watchbird-ojs-tamu.tdl.org/watchbird/article/view/1375 |journal=AFA Watchbird |language=en |volume=26 |issue=5 |pages=50–51}}</ref>

== Distribution and habitat==
The yellow-breasted magpie is native to Asia, and can be found in China, Laos, Thailand and Vietnam. It can tolerate altitudes of up to {{Convert|1500|m|ft|sp=us}} and they span over a region of about {{Convert|1920000|km2|abbr=on}}. They stay in these areas all year long and do not migrate.<ref name=IUCN/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Wenxing |first1=Long |last2=Runguo |first2=Zang |last3=Yi |first3=Ding |date=2011-11-15 |title=Community characteristics of tropical montane evergreen forest and tropical montane dwarf forest in Bawangling National Nature Reserve on Hainan Island, South China |url=https://www.biodiversity-science.net/EN/10.3724/SP.J.1003.2011.09309 |journal=Biodiversity Science |volume=19 |issue=5 |pages=558–566 |doi=10.3724/sp.j.1003.2011.09309 |doi-broken-date=1 July 2025 |issn=1005-0094|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lau |first1=Michael |last2=Hau |first2=Billy |last3=Chau |first3=Lawrence |last4=Siu |first4=Gloria |date=2000 |title=The present status and conservation of the biodiversity in Hong Kong |journal=Biodiversity Science |volume=08 |issue=1 |pages=25–35 |doi=10.17520/biods.2000004 |issn=1005-0094|doi-access=free }}</ref>

It can be found in moist forests within the tropical and subtropical regions of their distribution. Due to their bright green plumage, they can be seen easily when jumping from branch to branch in foliage. Therefore this is why they mainly occur in the tree canopies of forests, as this camouflages their green plumage better, protecting them from predation.<ref name=":1" />

== Behavior ==
This passerine bird is very loud and loves to hang out in the foliage of its forest habitat. They have been spotted alone, in pairs, or in small groups. They can be sometimes seen in mixed flocks with [[Laughingthrushes|laughingthrushes (''Garrulax leucolophus'')]] and [[drongo]]s.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-03-27 |title=Indochinese Green Magpie in China |url=https://www.shanghaibirding.com/birds-of-china/indochinese-green-magpie/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=Shanghai Birding 上海观鸟 |language=en-US}}</ref> The Indochinese green magpie does not migrate and stays in its native range. It has a mainly carnivorous diet and eats lot of different things. An adult Indochinese green magpie once lived for 18 years in captivity.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Madge |first1=Steve |title=Javan Green-Magpie (Cissa thalassina) |date=2020-03-04 |work=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |last2=Sharpe |first2=Chris |last3=Christie |first3=David|editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana |doi=10.2173/bow.shtmag1.01 }}</ref> When hunting their prey, they stalk it, corner it and then the male will make the kill.<ref name=":3" />

=== Vocalizations ===
Much like other corvids, the Indochinese green magpie is a [[songbird]] and has a very good and precise control of its [[Syrinx (bird anatomy)|syrinx]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2021-06-17 |title=What Is a Songbird, Exactly? {{!}} Audubon |url=https://www.audubon.org/news/what-songbird-exactly |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en}}</ref> It can produce sounds like noisy chattering, rasping notes, screeches, and ringing whistles.<ref name=":1" /> They can generate high-pitched series of notes that sounds like "po-puueeee-chuk", followed by lower pitched "eeeoooeeep groak".<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Madge |first=Steve |date=2020 |title=Indochinese Green-Magpie (Cissa hypoleuca), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/yebmag1/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.yebmag1.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Food and feeding===
The yellow-breasted magpie can eat a lot of different things, as it is carnivorous. Its diet includes small frogs, insects, eggs of snakes and lizards, and nestlings.{{cn|date=February 2025}} The insects from its diet eat a lot of [[lutein]]-rich plants, which is a yellow [[carotenoid]] pigment. This component is what makes the birds from the ''Cissa'' genus go from blue to bright green. Malnourished birds can be found to be more of a blue color due to the lack of the lutein pigment, since its body does not produce it.<ref>{{Cite web |title=When Javan Green Magpies feel blue – Silent Forest |url=https://www.silentforest.eu/when-javan-green-magpies-feel-blue/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=www.silentforest.eu}}</ref>

=== Breeding===
The month of May appears to be the month when their breeding is recorded. They were found to lay approximately 4 eggs in captivity. They place their nest hidden in a tree at a height of {{Convert|2-3|m|ft|sp=us}} above the ground. Their nest resembles a bowl made of small branches.{{cn|date=February 2025}} Its [[Generation time|generation length]] is of 6.7 years.<ref name=IUCN/>

== Conservation ==
The Indochinese green magpie has been assessed to be of least concern in 2016 by the [[IUCN Red List|IUCN red list]].<ref name=IUCN/> Its current population trend is declining, but the decline is not pronounced enough for them to be considered vulnerable. The decline is caused partially by habitat destruction in Hainan.<ref name=IUCN/> Another reason would be that the Indochinese green magpie is targeted in the illegal pet trade. Its bright and beautiful colors often makes it an ideal exotic pet, especially as it is very appreciated by birdwatchers.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-05-19 |title=The Indochinese Green Magpie is the 14,000th Species to Join the National Geographic Photo Ark |url=https://blog.nationalgeographic.org/2023/05/19/the-indochinese-green-magpie-is-the-14000th-species-to-join-the-national-geographic-photo-ark/ |access-date=2023-10-19 |website=National Geographic Society Newsroom |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tan |first1=Xiaocai |last2=Yan |first2=Peihao |last3=Liu |first3=Zongyue |last4=Qin |first4=Huan |last5=Jiang |first5=Aiwu |date=2023-10-01 |title=Demographics, behaviours, and preferences of birdwatchers and their implications for avitourism and avian conservation: A case study of birding in Nonggang, Southern China |journal=Global Ecology and Conservation |volume=46 |article-number=e02552 |doi=10.1016/j.gecco.2023.e02552 |issn=2351-9894|doi-access=free |bibcode=2023GEcoC..4602552T }}</ref> It is difficult to gauge the concurrent status of conservation of the Indochinese green magpie as we have not been able to quantify its global population size.<ref name=IUCN/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Thewlis |first1=R. M. |last2=Timmins |first2=R. J. |last3=Evans |first3=T. D. |last4=Duckworth |first4=J. W. |date=December 1998 |title=The conservation status of birds in Laos: a review of key species |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=8 |issue=S1 |pages=1–159 |doi=10.1017/s0959270900002197 |issn=0959-2709|doi-access=free }}</ref>

zh.extract:
黄胸绿鹊（学名：Cissa hypoleuca），又名东方绿鹊，是鸦科绿鹊属的一种，分布于越南、泰国、老挝、中国大陆和柬埔寨。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
黄胸绿鹊的栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林，主要栖息于山地的原始和次生林中。


亚种
黄胸绿鹊西南亚种（学名：Cissa hypoleuca jini）。在中国大陆，分布于四川、广西等地。该物种的模式产地在广西瑶山。
黄胸绿鹊海南亚种（学名：Cissa hypoleuca katsumatae）。在中国大陆，分布于海南等地。该物种的模式产地在海南五指山。
（学名：Cissa hypoleuca chauleti）
（学名：Cissa hypoleuca concolor）
（学名：Cissa hypoleuca hypoleuca）
